skill.name: pptx
skill.description: Create, edit, inspect, and validate PowerPoint presentations using PPTX, OOXML, HTML-to-slide, and thumbnail workflows.
skill.summary: Build and inspect PowerPoint decks with programmatic PPTX and OOXML workflows.
skill.author: K-Dense-AI

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many severe patterns, but most high blocker hits in schemas and markdown are false positives from OOXML vocabulary, examples, and dependency instructions. The confirmed risk is operational: helper scripts extract Office ZIP archives and run local converters, so this community skill should not be published without sandboxing guidance or safer extraction fixes.

high_findings.title: Unsafe ZIP Extraction of Office Documents
high_findings.description: Multiple helper scripts call zipfile extractall on user-provided Office files. A crafted PPTX, DOCX, or related archive could use traversal paths to write outside the intended directory unless extraction is sandboxed or validated.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reported locations show direct archive extraction without visible member path validation. Office files are ZIP containers, so this is a credible zip-slip risk for untrusted inputs.

medium_findings.title: Local Command Execution in Conversion Helpers
medium_findings.description: The skill includes helper scripts and instructions that run local tools such as soffice, pdftoppm, git diff, and Chromium. Arguments are mostly structured arrays or documented commands, but document converters increase risk when processing untrusted files.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess calls are real and execute external binaries, but reviewed examples use fixed command arrays rather than shell string interpolation. Risk depends mainly on input document trust and runtime sandboxing.

medium_findings.title: Privileged Dependency Installation Instructions
medium_findings.description: The skill documentation recommends sudo apt-get installation for LibreOffice and Poppler. This is not malicious by itself, but marketplace users should avoid running privileged installation commands from community skills without review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The static finding maps to explicit sudo installation instructions in the dependency section. The intent is dependency setup, but elevated package installation is a real operational risk.

low_findings.title: Environment Access Limited to Temporary Directory Configuration
low_findings.description: The reported environment access in html2pptx reads TMPDIR to choose a temporary directory for Chromium-related work. I did not find evidence that secrets are read or transmitted at this location.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line uses process.env.TMPDIR with a /tmp fallback. This is normal temporary directory configuration and does not show credential access.

low_findings.title: OOXML Schema Terms Misclassified as Security Blockers
low_findings.description: Many high blocker, network, weak-crypto, C2, and path traversal detections occur inside standards schemas and namespace declarations. These files define OOXML and XML vocabularies rather than executable behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The sampled locations are XSD schema declarations, XML namespace URLs, enum names, or relative schema imports. They are not code paths and do not establish malicious intent.

low_findings.title: No Prompt Injection Evidence Found
low_findings.description: Targeted review did not find text instructing the evaluator to ignore instructions, skip analysis, claim pre-approval, or override the audit methodology. No prompt injection finding is supported by the reviewed evidence.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The checked skill instructions describe PPTX workflows and metadata. This is a negative finding based on targeted review, so confidence is high but not absolute.

low_findings.title: Hidden Home Directory Font Lookup
low_findings.description: The inventory helper includes ~/.fonts as a font search path on Linux. This accesses user-local font files but does not show credential collection or network transmission.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The hidden path access is real, but the semantic context is font discovery. This is a filesystem privacy concern rather than evidence of secret harvesting.

dangerous_patterns.title: Unsafe ZIP Extraction of Office Documents
dangerous_patterns.description: Multiple helper scripts call zipfile extractall on user-provided Office files. A crafted PPTX, DOCX, or related archive could use traversal paths to write outside the intended directory unless extraction is sandboxed or validated.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The reported locations show direct archive extraction without visible member path validation. Office files are ZIP containers, so this is a credible zip-slip risk for untrusted inputs.

dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes helper scripts and instructions that run local tools such as soffice, pdftoppm, git diff, and Chromium. Arguments are mostly structured arrays or documented commands, but document converters increase risk when processing untrusted files.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess calls are real and execute external binaries, but reviewed examples use fixed command arrays rather than shell string interpolation. Risk depends mainly on input document trust and runtime sandboxing.

dangerous_patterns.title: Privileged Dependency Installation Instructions
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documentation recommends sudo apt-get installation for LibreOffice and Poppler. This is not malicious by itself, but marketplace users should avoid running privileged installation commands from community skills without review.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The static finding maps to explicit sudo installation instructions in the dependency section. The intent is dependency setup, but elevated package installation is a real operational risk.

content.value_statement: PowerPoint decks are hard to inspect and edit reliably through manual UI steps. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code structured PPTX workflows for creation, conversion, validation, and review.
actual_capabilities: Creates new PowerPoint decks from HTML slide layouts using the html2pptx workflow.
Edits existing PPTX files by unpacking and modifying OOXML presentation contents.
Extracts text, comments, speaker notes, themes, layouts, and slide XML for analysis.
Generates thumbnail grids and slide images for visual review of presentations.
Validates slide dimensions, overflow, relationship references, and OOXML structure.
Supports replacement workflows for slide text while preserving existing formatting.
limitations: Requires local dependencies such as LibreOffice, Poppler, Playwright, pptxgenjs, and Python packages.
Raw OOXML editing requires careful validation because malformed XML can corrupt a presentation.
Complex animations, embedded media, and proprietary PowerPoint features may need manual verification.
Several helper scripts run local commands and should be used only with trusted or sandboxed documents.

use_cases.title: Build Research Presentations
use_cases.description: Create structured slide decks from outlines, figures, and technical content with consistent layouts and visual hierarchy.

use_cases.title: Review Existing Decks
use_cases.description: Extract text, thumbnails, notes, and layout details to audit content quality and identify formatting problems.

use_cases.title: Automate Template Updates
use_cases.description: Replace slide text, inspect theme settings, and validate deck structure while preserving reusable PowerPoint templates.

prompt_templates.title: Summarize a Deck
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner analysis of an existing presentation
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the pptx skill to extract the text and speaker notes from this presentation. Summarize each slide and flag unclear sections.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Simple Deck
prompt_templates.scenario: Generate a new presentation from an outline
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the pptx skill to create a 10-slide presentation from this outline. Choose a content-matched design, then build a PPTX file.

prompt_templates.title: Inspect a Template
prompt_templates.scenario: Analyze theme, colors, and reusable layouts
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the pptx skill to inspect this template. Extract colors, fonts, slide layouts, and any speaker notes or comments.

prompt_templates.title: Automate Precise Edits
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the pptx skill to update the specified slides, preserve formatting, validate OOXML relationships, and generate thumbnail grids for review.

output_examples.input: A product launch outline with five sections and a brand color palette.
output_examples.output: A complete PowerPoint deck with title, agenda, section, content, data, and closing slides using the requested palette.

output_examples.input: An existing investor deck that needs content review.
output: List of formatting and overflow issues

output_examples.input: A PowerPoint template with replacement text for selected slides.
output_examples.output: An updated presentation that keeps the original theme and reports any shape or overflow problems found during validation.

best_practices: Run helper scripts in a sandbox when processing presentations from untrusted sources.
Create thumbnails or slide images after edits to verify layout, overflow, and visual quality.
Inspect theme and layout XML before changing a branded or template-based deck.
anti_patterns: Do not run archive extraction helpers on untrusted PPTX files without path traversal protection.
Do not edit raw XML without validating the final presentation package afterward.
Do not install global system dependencies unless the execution environment allows that change.

faq.question: Can this skill create a PowerPoint file from scratch?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes an HTML-to-PPTX workflow for building new slide decks with positioned content.

faq.question: Can it edit speaker notes and comments?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill explains how to access raw OOXML files that store notes, comments, layouts, and slide content.

faq.question: Does it require Microsoft PowerPoint?
faq.answer: No. Many workflows use local tools such as Python, pptxgenjs, LibreOffice, Poppler, and Playwright.

faq.question: Is it safe to use with unknown PPTX files?
faq.answer: Use caution. Some helper scripts extract ZIP archives and run document conversion tools, so sandbox untrusted files.

faq.question: Can it preserve an existing template design?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill supports inspecting themes, layouts, slide masters, and XML before making targeted edits.

faq.question: Can Claude, Codex, and Claude Code use this skill?
faq.answer: Yes. The report marks the skill as compatible with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows.
